Goal¶

I wrote this code to strip text from an image. I've always wanted to learn how to do this. It just seemed cool... and useful. My approach below is to get a look at what I want to strip. Identify the areas of the image I want to strip. Get the info from those areas into python. Then get all the info into one dataset so I can do some analysis on it down the line.

First, I just want to take a look at the image I want to strip. I'll also use this opportunity to set some pathing and image type variables that we'll want to use throughout the project.

In [1]:
from PIL import Image, ImageFilter
from PIL.ImageFilter import (
   BLUR, CONTOUR, DETAIL, EDGE_ENHANCE, EDGE_ENHANCE_MORE,
   EMBOSS, FIND_EDGES, SMOOTH, SMOOTH_MORE, SHARPEN
)
imgPath = r'C:\Users\nickp\OneDrive\Desktop\Home Data Projects\Viz\mtb-viz-challenge\Waterfall chart\\'
imgType = '.png'
imgBase = '4eb13a20-989d-4943-b827-f3855acc32a8'
img = Image.open(imgPath+imgBase+imgType)
img = img.filter(SHARPEN)
img = img.filter(SMOOTH)
img.show()

Now that we have a grasp on what the image looks like, we need to get it ready to text strip. We use the OpenCV library to do this. This library will change the image into a numpy array. We'll convert it to a black and white image as it increases the probability of a clean text strip.

In [2]:
import numpy as np
import cv2

npImg = cv2.imread(imgPath+imgBase+imgType)
npImg = cv2.cvtColor(npImg, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
cv2.imshow('Original Image (Grayscale)',npImg)
# Waits for a keystroke
cv2.waitKey(0) 
# Destroys all the windows created
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

Next, we're going to make it easier to identify the areas of the image we want to strip the text from.

To make it cleaner, we can use plotly and the info we got from converting the image to a numpy area with the OpenCV library. It will seem a bit clunkly maybe, but all we're really doing is making our image the background of a graph, then plotting our axes and gridlines based on the image coordinates we identified with OpenCV.

From that numpy array presentation of our image, we'll set our Y axis with the image height, and the X axis with image width. For sizing purposes, we'll scale everything to .10 of the numpy height and width. We then flip the image over the Y axis so the values correspond with height as we expect. We just have to remember that a -42 on our Y axis = a height of 420 on our image. Last, we'll do some formatting of the image so it's a bit easier to identify the strip areas (phrasing).

In [3]:
npHeight = npImg.shape[0]
npWidth = npImg.shape[1]
print('Height (Y) = ' + str(npHeight))
print('Width (X) = ' + str(npWidth))

Height (Y) = 1440
Width (X) = 2560
In [5]:
import plotly.graph_objects as go
grphX = npWidth / 10
grphY = npHeight / 10
#Size and space background image on graph
back = go.Layout(images= [dict(
        source= img,
        xref= "x",
        yref= "y",
        x= 0,
        y= 0,
        sizex= grphX,
        sizey= grphY,
        sizing= "stretch", opacity= 1, layer= "below",
    )
        ]
)
fig=go.Figure(data=[], layout=back)
fig.update_layout(
    width = npWidth * 1.5,
    height = npHeight * 1.5,
    font_family = "Arial",
    title = "Where Do You Want to Crop?"
)
fig.update_xaxes(range=[0, grphX], showline=True, linewidth=1, linecolor='rgb(102,255,102)', mirror=True
                ,showticklabels=True, gridcolor = 'rgb(102,255,102)', gridwidth = 1, dtick=1)
fig.update_yaxes(range=[grphY * -1, 0], showline=True, linewidth=1, linecolor='rgb(102,255,102)', mirror=True
                 ,showticklabels=True, gridcolor = 'rgb(102,255,102)', gridwidth = 1, dtick=1)
fig.show()
fig.save()

Ok! Now we identify the areas of the image that contain the data we wanted include in our eventual data frame. Then we'll loop over the images in our folder, grabbing the part of the photo that has the numbers we want.

In [6]:
import os

for entry in os.scandir(imgPath):
    if entry.is_file():
        print(f"Processing file: {entry.name}")

In [7]:
import os
snipsPath = r'C:\Users\nickp\OneDrive\Desktop\Home Data Projects\Viz\mtb-viz-challenge\Waterfall chart\snips\\'

y1 = [650]
y2 = [1200]
x1 = [2000]
x2 = [2400]
for entry in os.scandir(imgPath):
    imgLoop = entry.name
    for (a, b, c, d) in zip(y1, y2, x1, x2):
        npImgLoop = cv2.imread(imgPath+imgLoop)
        npImgLoop = cv2.cvtColor(npImgLoop, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
        crop = npImgLoop[a:b, c:d]
        cv2.imwrite(snipsPath+imgLoop, crop)

error: OpenCV(4.5.4) D:\a\opencv-python\opencv-python\opencv\modules\imgproc\src\color.cpp:182: error: (-215:Assertion failed) !_src.empty() in function 'cv::cvtColor'

Next, we're going to strip the text from the images we just created! We need to import pystessaract (make sure you have it installed first). Then we will take a look at the individual images we want to take text from. In doing this we can identify if there are any oddities we need to clean up down the line.

We have several levels where we can handle any data discrepancies. The first is in the image strip settings from pytesseract. There's various segmentation options set with the --psm variable. You can test these options to see what best fits your data set. --psm 4 was a good fit for the data we're using here. Next, you can do data clean up in the strip and clean process. We'll be doing that below. Finally, you can do clean up and data type conversions as needed after you get your data into your working dataframe. We'll be doing that below as well.

In [8]:
import pytesseract
pytesseract.pytesseract.tesseract_cmd = r'C:\Program Files\Tesseract-OCR\tesseract.exe'
In [11]:
# Cut out part from each image that we want to read data from
picData = []
picNames = []
tessConfig = '--psm 4 --oem 3 -l eng'

for entry in os.scandir(snipsPath):
    stripImgName = entry.name
    stripImg = cv2.imread(snipsPath+stripImgName)
    stripValues = pytesseract.image_to_data(stripImg,config=tessConfig, output_type='data.frame')
    picData.append(stripValues)
    
    x = 0
    while x < len(stripValues):
        picNames.append(stripImgName)
        x +=1
In [12]:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.concat(picData, ignore_index=True)
df['pic_name'] = picNames
In [13]:
# Review for any adjustments that need to be made
df.to_excel('review_arc_raids.xlsx')
df

In [14]:
# Loop through to get values and do some minor clean up
value_check = df['text'].tolist()
return_type = []
location = []
return_result = []
pic_name_list = []

for v in value_check:
    av = None
    if str(v).upper() in 'RETURNED':
        av = 'Returned Safely'
        return_type.append(av)
    elif str(v).upper() in 'KNOCKED':
        av = 'Knocked Out'
        return_type.append(av)
    elif str(v).upper() == 'DAM':
        av = 'Dam Battlegrounds'
        location.append(av)
    elif str(v).upper() in 'BURIED':
        av = 'Buried City'
        location.append(av)
    elif str(v).upper() in 'SPACEPORT':
        av = 'Spaceport'
        location.append(av)
    elif str(v).upper() in 'BLUE':
        av = 'Blue Gate'
        location.append(av)
    elif str(v)[-3:].isnumeric() == True:
        av = str(v).replace(",","")
        return_result.append(av)
    else:
        av
In [15]:
# Clean up numbers, note, addtl adjustments for extra number and negative handling done in reporting layer
import re

cleaned_return_result = [re.sub(r'[^0-9]', '', s) for s in return_result]
cleaned_return_result

In [16]:
# Create csv for dashboard
import csv

header = ['return_type', 'return_locatiion', 'return_result', 'pic_name']

combined_data = zip(return_type, location, cleaned_return_result)

with open("arc_raids.csv", "w", newline='') as csvfile:
    writer = csv.writer(csvfile)
    writer.writerow(header) # Write the header row
    writer.writerows(combined_data) # Write all the data rows
